# Questions in Livestream 29

Live Stream #29: A Copper Penny For Your Thoughts

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12</a> | Has he thought about doing book reviews, and what does he think about magic systems in fiction, such as the *Kane Chronicles*? | He said he does book reviews for peer-reviewed journals, but not for YouTube because they take too much time and do not fit the medium well. Fictional magic systems vary: some are more compatible with a Christian worldview than others, depending on whether they work within creation's order or disrupt it. | He explained that formal book reviews are something he does for peer-reviewed journals, but YouTube reviews would take too much time and are not a strong fit for an audiovisual platform. On fictional magic, he had not read the *Kane Chronicles*, but said the question depends on the author's metaphysics: a system like Tolkien's, where abilities extend a created nature, can be more compatible with a Christian worldview than a disruptive system such as Harry Potter's. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=385" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:25</a> | How annoying is it when historians argue Moses was invented because "Moses" is an Egyptian word? | Very annoying. He treated that argument as badly informed and almost joked it was a 9.5 out of 10 on the annoyance scale. | He answered almost entirely by rating the argument's annoyance level at nine or 9.5 out of ten, with laughter. The brevity of the reply itself shows that he considered the claim unserious rather than something needing extended rebuttal in that moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:57</a> | What does he know about the historicity of Darius the Mede? | He said the evidence is thin outside scripture, but he regarded the proposal that Darius the Mede was another name for Cyrus as at least plausible. | He said Scripture is the only source he knows for Darius the Mede, but he rejected making an absence-of-evidence argument from that. He cited Donald J. Wiseman's suggestion that ancient Near Eastern kings could have multiple names, comparing Tiglath-Pileser also being called Pul, and treated the idea that Darius the Mede was another name for Cyrus as a plausible working hypothesis rather than a certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=558" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:18</a> | Is the James ossuary good evidence, and could it really be tied to Jesus or the Talpiot tomb? | He was very skeptical. Because of provenance and forgery concerns, he said it is not reliable evidence and is not needed to establish Jesus as a historical person. | He emphasized that the James ossuary is controversial because it was held by someone associated with forgeries, and because sampling from the inscription raised questions about modern material. Although he said many accept part of the inscription as original, the contested addition, cleaning explanation, and general irregularities make it unusable as good evidence. He added that the historical existence of Jesus does not depend on it because other extrabiblical evidence is sufficient. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=731" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:11</a> | How would he answer the claim that Ezekiel falsely prophesied about Nebuchadnezzar destroying Tyre? | He rejected that criticism. He argued the case ignores both Phoenician and cuneiform evidence that Tyre was in fact subdued by Babylon. | He said the objection misstates the evidence, especially by treating the long siege as though it came directly from the Bible rather than Josephus using Phoenician sources. He pointed to Phoenician documentation and cuneiform tablets indicating Babylonian siege activity and Tyre becoming a vassal state, so he regarded the claim that Nebuchadnezzar did not subdue Tyre as historically bizarre. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=1044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:24</a> | Did Egypt lose contact with Nubia after dynasty 20, and how could Shishak recruit Cushites? | He said Egypt never fully lost contact with Kush. The biblical Cushites are not simply "Nubians," and contact with Kush continued long after dynasty 20. | He distinguished Cushites from Nubians and said 2 Chronicles is referring to Kushites, whose region lay between Thebes and Nubia rather than simply to Nubians farther south. Egypt, in his view, never lost contact with Kush any more than it lost contact with Sinai, and some contact with Nubia also continued at least into the period of dynasty 25. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=1257" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:57</a> | What were the plagues in Genesis 12:17, who was that pharaoh, and did he wrong Abraham? | The text does not specify the plagues. He said the pharaoh's personal name is unknown and treated the king in that story as acting more righteously than Abram. | He said Genesis does not identify the plagues, so they cannot be specified from the text. He also treated "pharaoh" as a retrojected later title for the king of Egypt, not a personal name, and said the story likely reflects an earlier period than the title's later usage. In the narrative, he thought the Egyptian king is legitimately angry because Abram deceived him, making the king an example of a righteous Gentile while Abram is the one acting wrongly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=1511" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:11</a> | What is his view of Israel Finkelstein's work? | He said Finkelstein's older work was influential, but he thinks his minimalist skepticism has been overtaken by later archaeology. | He described Finkelstein as once highly influential in Israeli archaeology, but said his continued skepticism about the Davidic and Solomonic kingdoms now looks dated. In his judgment, Israeli archaeology has moved beyond Finkelstein's minimalist position, and continued discoveries have made that older skepticism less persuasive even if some of his books still contain useful material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=1653" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:33</a> | What is his take on radiocarbon dating? | He said it can still be useful for relative dating, but he does not trust it as a precise absolute dating tool in the ancient Near East. | He said radiocarbon dating has real limits in the ancient Near East, where correlations with dendrochronology are difficult because trees do not form rings in the same way. He still sees value in comparing similar samples to determine which is older, but rejects using radiocarbon as if it can provide precise absolute dates there, citing cases where radiocarbon-driven reconstructions conflict with historical records. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=1913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:53</a> | How far did Ramesses III campaign in the Levant, and would that have affected Israel during the judges period? | He said Ramesses III's campaigns were largely coastal and would not have directly affected the Israelite tribes in the Judean foothills. | He said Ramesses III's campaigns were limited mostly to the coastal plain, as were many campaigns after Merneptah. Because the Israelite tribes were in the Judean foothills, separated from the coast by difficult geography and swamps, an Egyptian army would usually bypass them unless the area became worth the trouble. He therefore did not think these campaigns affected the judges-period tribes directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=2092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:52</a> | Did the Canaanite pantheon begin as one god with many titles? | He said he was not persuaded. In his view, the evidence still looks more like real polytheism or at least henotheism than an original single-god system. | He framed the idea as part of broader theories about religions evolving either from animism toward monotheism or in the reverse direction. For Ugaritic and Phoenician evidence, he did not think the texts support a single deity later split into many titles or gods; he pointed especially to Canaanite mythic material such as the Baal cycle as evidence for genuine polytheism, or at least henotheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=2511" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:51</a> | Was the Exodus a military operation with Moses as general, as Richard Gabriel argued on the History Channel? | He rejected that reconstruction. He said Moses was likely no more than a scribe, the despoiling of Egypt was not a raid, and Gabriel's reading defies the text. | He criticized the History Channel framing and said the Exodus was not a military operation. Moses, in his reconstruction, was probably no more than a scribe associated with an elite household rather than a general, and the despoiling of Egypt was not an armed sweep through Egyptian settlements. He read it as Israelites asking neighbors for provisions and receiving them because they had found favor with the Egyptians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=2724" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:24</a> | Which parts of the Old Testament have Egyptian vocabulary, and what are examples of Egyptian loan words? | He said Egyptian loan words are especially common in the Torah and then taper off later. He gave examples such as words for river, linen, reeds, lapis, and the basket or chest used for Moses and Noah. | He said Egyptian loanwords occur most heavily in the Torah, with some continuing into Joshua and Judges before tapering off in later historical and prophetic books. His examples included words connected with the river, linen, reeds, lapis, and the word *tebah* for the basket or chest used of Moses' basket and Noah's ark. He added that the shift toward other loanword sources in later books fits the biblical narrative's later historical settings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=2926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:46</a> | Did average Egyptian farmers do anything recreational, or did they just survive? | He said farmers were extremely poor and hard to study directly, but they may have played simple music and games when time and resources allowed. | He stressed that farmers were very poor and are difficult to study because they left almost no written records and their settlements were affected by inundation cycles. Still, he thought recreation was possible in limited forms: farmers could make simple flutes from papyrus reeds, play music in the evening, and perhaps play games, though evidence for the farming class specifically is scarce. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=3132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:12</a> | What view best explains the large Exodus numbers, and how do we know those numbers were inflated? | He said he is not settled, though the "aleph" explanation has some credibility. He argued ancient Near Eastern numbers are often inflated, the two-million figure comes from interpretation, and settlement geography limits what the population could have been. | He said he has not settled on one explanation, though reading *aleph* as a clan or military unit has some credibility. His reasons for suspecting inflation include the broader ancient Near Eastern habit of inflated numbers and the geography of sites such as Avaris, whose size could not support the later interpretive figure of two million people. He also distinguished the textual 600,000 from later calculations that multiply it into men, women, children, and elders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=3696" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:36</a> | Do Jewish traditions about blue sapphire or lapis tablets make sense as Egyptian symbolism for the Ten Commandments? | He said sapphire and lapis were prized but not likely tablet materials for Sinai; sapphire was not locally available, lapis was imported in small pieces, and granite or limestone would fit the geology better. | He said sapphire and lapis were valuable stones, but not good candidates for Sinai tablets. He did not think Egyptians had a local source for modern blue sapphire, and lapis was imported from Afghanistan, usually in small pieces rather than tablet-sized slabs. Given Sinai's geology, he thought granite or limestone would be much more likely and would work well as writing material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=3860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:20</a> | How should claims about J, E, and P source theology be explained when one source is said to use anthropomorphic or primitive language and another transcendent language? | He said this does not need to be explained by multiple authors. In his view, the burden is on critics to prove that such changes in tone require different sources. | He answered that variety in tone or depiction can be explained by the virtuosity of the author rather than by separate sources. He argued that critics bear the burden of proving that anthropomorphic language or a different theological register requires a different author, especially since comparable variation in other ancient texts is not always treated the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=4043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:23</a> | Which scholars give the best arguments for the unity of the flood story and Joseph story against mainstream source critics? | He recommended John Walton as useful, while noting he does not agree with everything Walton says. He also tied his own view of Pentateuchal unity to historical and literary context and Egyptian vocabulary in Genesis. | He recommended John Walton as someone who handles the unity question well, while making clear that he does not agree with Walton on everything. He connected that recommendation to an approach that reads the stories in historical and literary context, and said his own view of Pentateuchal unity is supported by Egyptian vocabulary and contextual features in Genesis that point to early source material or composition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=4714" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:34</a> | Does he have information on the game Senet, including names or rules, for someone coding the game? | He said the rules were never written down and modern Senet rules are reconstructed guesswork. He could translate Egyptian words on a board, but cannot supply ancient rules with confidence. | He said Senet is frustrating because boards survive but the ancient rules were not written down. Modern rules are reconstructions, closer to educated guesswork than secure scholarship, so a coder would need to rely on reconstructed play unless better evidence appears. He offered that Egyptian words on a board could be translated, but not that the original rule set could be recovered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=4899" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:39</a> | If a basic box was only for the wealthy, could ordinary Egyptians not make one themselves? | He said the main issue was not just skill but scarce, expensive wood. Wood was valuable enough that larger pieces went to trained craftsmen who could conserve the material and avoid waste. | He said the limiting factor was access to wood, not merely whether someone could shape a box. Trees were uncommon in Egypt, so wood was expensive and associated with high-value uses such as coffins and furniture. Larger pieces were entrusted to trained craftsmen because they could make several objects from one log and conserve even scraps for inlays, making waste unacceptable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=5084" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:44</a> | Did Moses have a second wife, or is Zipporah the Cushite woman in Numbers 12:1, and why did Aaron and Miriam speak against Moses because of her? | He argued Zipporah was not the Cushite woman because Midianites and Cushites were distinct groups. He thought Numbers 12 may involve a second unnamed wife and a deeper dispute about access to the tent of meeting. | He said Zipporah was a Midianite, not a Cushite, and that the two groups were geographically and tribally distinct enough that an Egyptian-context audience would not confuse them. He considered it possible that Moses was widowed and remarried, leaving the Cushite woman unnamed. For Aaron and Miriam's complaint, he suggested the wife may have been a pretext for a deeper dispute about authority and who could enter the tent of meeting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=5385" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:45</a> | Could Egyptian farmers still afford to be married? | He said yes. Farmers typically married within their class, and a wife also contributed labor to the farm, so marriage could increase the household's working capacity. | He said farmers did marry, usually within the farming class. Because farmers worked licensed plots and women also labored in the fields, marriage could increase a household's productive capacity rather than merely add expense. He also noted the low social status of farmers and said marriage patterns generally kept people within that class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=5615" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:35</a> | What was the Egyptian view of left- and right-handedness, and did Egyptians or local peoples have a sign language for the deaf? | He said there is no clear evidence for a sign language for the deaf, and Egyptians probably did not accommodate deafness well. Right-handedness was treated as normal, while left-handedness was noted as unusual but not necessarily evil. | He said he did not know of evidence for a deaf sign language, and suspected deaf people and others with many disabilities received little accommodation, unlike dwarfs, who could hold respected roles. On handedness, Egyptians recognized right and left; right-handedness was treated as normal, while left-handedness was noted as unusual or unnatural but not as morally evil in the later "sinister" sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=5840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:20</a> | Why does he think Abraham was an Amorite, and would that create theological problems since Israel later fought Amorites? | He pointed to biblical descent language and Abraham's northern Mesopotamian background. He said it creates no theological problem because ancient alliances and conflicts could change quickly between related groups. | He cited biblical language about Israel's descent and Abraham's association with northern Mesopotamia, an area he identified with Amorite occupation. He saw no theological problem in Israel later fighting Amorites because ancient Near Eastern alliances were unstable: related groups could intermarry, make treaties, cooperate, and then become enemies within a generation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=6180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:00</a> | Did ancient Egypt ever trade with Asia under Solomon? | He said there is evidence for trade between Egypt and Asia, including material links reaching India and later evidence for broader Asian connections. Solomon would have profited by taxing caravans through his territory. | He said there is evidence of Egypt-Asia trade from at least the Late Bronze Age, including cloves from India in Egyptian contexts and later evidence such as silk. In Solomon's period, he argued, trade routes between Egypt and Asia would pass through territory Solomon controlled, letting him tax caravans and profit from that traffic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=6425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:05</a> | Based on the table of contents for Doug Petrovich's upcoming *Origins of the Hebrews*, has Petrovich made any new early-Exodus arguments that have not already been addressed? | He said he had not seen a worthwhile new argument and was very skeptical of the book's publisher, peer-review situation, and argument quality. | He said he saw no argument from Petrovich that had not already been adequately addressed, and he expected the book to be poor. His skepticism focused on the publishing route, lack of meaningful peer review, the use of a creationist press he could not verify well, and what he regarded as the weak potential of the arguments themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=6759" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:39</a> | Do Adam and Eve precede Sumerian culture, Gobekli Tepe, or Jiroft culture, or are they Sumerian? | He said he affirms a historical Adam and Eve but thinks they long predate Sumer and probably even Gobekli Tepe; beyond written sources, the historical reconstruction becomes very murky. | He said he affirms a historical Adam and Eve while also seeing them as literary archetypes. Historically, he thinks they long predate Sumer and may even predate Gobekli Tepe, but beyond the invention of writing the evidence becomes extremely limited. For cultures such as Gobekli Tepe, he stressed that archaeology gives artifacts and images but no texts explaining what happened. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=7014" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:54</a> | What were the ancient Egyptian punishments for adultery? | He said most adultery cases probably ended in divorce because divorce was easy. Severe punishments such as disfigurement or execution were rare and tied to egregious class transgressions. | He said adultery questions depended heavily on social class. Since men and women could generally initiate divorce, the normal consequence was likely divorce, sometimes with court involvement over property division. Severe punishments, such as cutting off the nose or ears, were exceptional and connected to egregious violations of class boundaries, such as a high-ranking woman committing adultery with a slave. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=7423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:43</a> | What makes theophanies in the Old Testament or other ancient cultures valid and real, and how did they make contact? | He said theophanies are rare and usually involve God or a spiritual being condescending to human communication, often through a human-like visage rather than incarnation. Other ancient Near Eastern evidence is too thin for a coherent comparison. | He said theophanies are rare in nearly every religion and especially scarce in Egyptian texts. In Israelite literature, he described them as direct visitations in which God or a spiritual being takes on a human-like visage, not true flesh or incarnation. He also allowed for other forms such as light or cloud imagery, but stressed that the spiritual being is condescending to human perception rather than becoming ordinary humanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=7828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:28</a> | When and where does the Egyptian flood myth involving Sekhmet and blood originate, and is it related to the plague of blood? | He said he does not think it is related to the plague of blood, though both may reflect rare Nile events. He was unsure about the myth's exact origin and said he would need more research. | He said the Sekhmet story is not a true flood myth in the same sense, but is tied to the Nile inundation and the deity being intoxicated and put to sleep. He did not know its exact origin without further research. He rejected a direct relationship to the plague of blood, though he allowed that both might reflect rare natural events where the Nile appeared bloodlike at the start of an unusually high inundation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x-ro6ZwbeMg?t=8058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:18</a> | Are the hieroglyphs in his background made with stamps pressed into soft clay? | He said no. They are carved into limestone in a Giza tomb, and the clay-like color comes from desert varnish rather than the material being soft clay. | He said the background image comes from a tomb on the Giza plateau, where hieroglyphs were carved into limestone rather than stamped into clay. The reddish or baked-clay look comes from desert varnish and exposure to the elements near a doorway or corridor, not from the material being soft clay. He identified the tomb context as near Khufu's pyramid and praised the decorated mastabas around the pyramids. |
